{"primary_mode":"summaries","declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"Use a budget-matched step schedule directly in build_scheduler so the fixed-step run does not stay effectively unscheduled. A simple OneCycleLR is the least invasive way to improve optimization speed without touching the rest of the training loop.","edits":[{"op":"replace_function","function":"build_scheduler","old":"","new":"","anchor":"","text":"","source":"def build_scheduler(optimizer: optim.Optimizer, steps_per_epoch: int):
    total_steps = TRAIN_MAX_STEPS if TRAIN_MAX_STEPS is not None else max(steps_per_epoch, 1) * 4
    return optim.lr_scheduler.OneCycleLR(
        optimizer,
        max_lr=LEARNING_RATE * 2.0,
        total_steps=total_steps,
        pct_start=0.2,
        anneal_strategy="cos",
        div_factor=10.0,
        final_div_factor=100.0,
    )"}]}

{"primary_mode":"indexing","declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["topk"],"rationale":"Keep the stronger AdamW + larger-batch setup, but make the optimizer more responsive for a 256-step run. Lower beta2 and a slightly higher learning rate are the most direct remaining knobs for short-horizon convergence.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.95)","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["indexing"],"rationale":"The last optimizer tweak moved in the wrong direction. Restore the better AdamW settings that already proved stronger, then increase the batch size to push more data through each of the fixed 256 updates.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.95)","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"layout","declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["micro"],"rationale":"The optimizer and batch setup are already strong, so the next low-risk move is to give the plain MLP one more hidden stage. This adds capacity without changing the verified training loop or schedule.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 2","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"layout","declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"The deeper plain MLP is working, so the next step is to widen it modestly while leaving the optimizer and schedule unchanged. A slightly larger first width should give the model more capacity without changing the training dynamics that already improved verification loss.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"layout","declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["micro"],"rationale":"The last gain came from giving the plain MLP more capacity, so the next low-risk move is to widen the same 3-layer structure rather than change the optimizer that is already working. This keeps the successful training regime intact while testing whether the model is still under-width.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"layout","declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["micro"],"rationale":"The wider 3-layer MLP is still improving, so the next cheapest test is a modest additional width increase rather than changing the optimizer or schedule again. This keeps the training dynamics intact while checking whether the model is still slightly under-capacity.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"layout","declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["micro"],"rationale":"The extra width at 24 was too aggressive, so go back to the better 20-wide setting and spend the capacity budget on one additional hidden layer instead. This keeps the proven width scale while testing whether depth still has room to help.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 3","new":"DEPTH = 4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["layout"],"rationale":"The extra depth did not pay off, so revert to the stronger 3-layer shape and spend the remaining budget on a larger batch. With a fixed step budget, a bigger batch can be a cleaner way to squeeze more information out of each update.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 4","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"The batch-size scaling was the strongest recent signal, so continue that path before changing the architecture or optimizer again. A larger batch should further reduce gradient noise while preserving the already-working 3-layer AdamW setup.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"Batch scaling has been the dominant win so far, and the current 768 batch still appears to be improving the fixed-step regime. Take one more step up in batch size before changing the well-performing optimizer or architecture.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"Batch scaling has been the most reliable improvement lever so far, and the current 1024 batch is still trending down. {"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"The batch-size increases have continued to help under the fixed-step budget, and 1536 still improved the verifier. {"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"Batch scaling is still the clearest positive trend, and 2048 continued to improve the verifier loss. {"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"The batch-size sweep is still producing steady gains, and the current 3072 batch beat the previous best. One more increase is the most direct way to test whether the model is still benefiting from reduced gradient noise.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 3072","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"The large-batch trend is still positive, and 4096 produced another measurable improvement. Take one more moderate batch increase before touching the optimizer or model width, which have been stable.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 6144","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"layout","declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":["micro"],"rationale":"The huge-batch regime is now clearly working, so the next plausible gain is to let the MLP use a bit more width under that stable optimization setup. This is the smallest capacity increase that still tests a real remaining bottleneck.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"primary_mode":"micro","declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","secondary_modes":[],"rationale":"Batch scaling has remained the most reliable signal all the way up to 6144, with each increase still shaving off loss.
